front: Work: Orgon hides under a table while the title character seduces his wife
Work: this play ends with a deus ex machina in which the king's officer arrests the title scheming religious hypocrite
Work: Richard Wilbur translated this play into English, preserving the rhyming couplets
Work: Valere tries to win the heart of Mariane, daughter of Orgon, while the title character tries to stop him
Work: the maid Dorine voices her opinion of a forced marriage but stops talking every time her master is about to slap her
Work: its premiere at the multi-day festival Pleasures of the Enchanted Island was scandalous
Work: Colley Cibber adapted this play as The Non-Juror
Work: this playwright died hours after performing as the hypochondriac Argan in this play
Work: the maid Toinette has the protagonist play dead, revealing that his wife Beline does not truly love him
Work: Toinette disguises herself as a doctor to fool the supposedly sickly protagonist, repeating 'ignoramus!' as her master lists foods
Work: Angelique marries Cleante (disguised as a music master) despite being promised to a doctor by her hypochondriac father
Work: the 'dormitive principle' logical fallacy was coined referencing a scene where characters try to explain how opium works
Work: the final scene is a mock-Latin ballet ceremony ending with 'Vivat, vivat, vivat, vivat'
Work: the quack doctor Thomas Diafoirus is in love with Angelique, the daughter of the title character
Work: Alceste insults Oronte's sonnet, leading to a lawsuit
Work: Alceste attempts to woo Celimene despite being the titular character who hates most people
Work: Arsinoe is told her criticisms of Celimene may be because she is simply too old
Work: Alceste goes into self-imposed exile after losing a lawsuit against Oronte
Work: Arnolphe tries to raise his ward Agnes in ignorance to make her his ideal wife
Work: in response to criticism of this play, the author wrote another play in which the critics themselves watch that play
Work: Arnolphe assumes the name 'Monsieur de la Souche' and gives his ward Agnes eleven maxims of marriage
Work: Monsieur Jourdain, son of a cloth merchant, aspires to join the aristocracy
Work: Cleonte disguises himself as a Turkish prince (speaking Sabir) to marry Jourdain's daughter Lucille
Work: the protagonist is surprised to learn he has been speaking prose his entire life in a famous exchange with his philosophy master
Work: the Music Master and Dancing Master stage ballet scenes for their social-climbing student
Work: Sganarelle is a woodcutter beaten and forced into becoming a doctor by his wife Martine's revenge
back: The Doctor in Spite of Himself (Moliere)
work: The Doctor in Spite of Himself

front: Work: a character sardonically observes that craftsmen pay for material they destroy, but doctors can butcher whomever they want
back: The Doctor in Spite of Himself (Moliere)
work: The Doctor in Spite of Himself

front: Work: Cathos and Magdelon put on airs of sophistication for their debut in Paris in this satire of the precieuses movement
Work: Mascarille recites a 'funny poem' with the punchline 'Stop thief! Stop thief!'
Work: Gorgibus curses 'novels, verses, songs, and sonnets' and vows to marry off his daughter and niece or send them to a nunnery
Work: this play is an adaptation of Plautus's Aulularia; a valet negotiates an unfair loan secretly from his master's father
Author: co-founded the Illustrious Theater Company; his troupe created the genre of the comedie-ballet
Author: Sganarelle is a recurring character in this playwright's works, often played by the author himself
Author: this playwright's death possibly originated the theatrical superstition against wearing green onstage
Author: his troupe made a permanent home in the Hotel du Petit-Bourbon after performing Corneille's Nicomede before Louis XIV
Author: collaborated with Corneille and Philippe Quinault on the comedy Psyche
Author: his acting company eventually evolved into the Comedie-Francaise
Author: Ariane Mnouchkine made a biopic of this playwright
Author: French theatre awards named for this playwright have been won by Yasmina Reza
Author: drew on Plautus in a play titled for this character, later adapted by Dryden and Kleist; the French word for 'look-alike' comes from the servant Sosie
Author: in this play, Sganarelle is falsely convinced his wife is unfaithful and advises the audience to 'never believe anything'
Author: inspired by Terence's Adelphoe, Ariste gives his prospective wife more freedom than Sganarelle in this play
